1.synthesis:
Samuel takes a flask of oil and pours it on Saul's head, kisses him, and says: "Has not the Lord anointed you ruler over his inheritance?" The anointing is private, between Samuel and Saul alone, before any public assembly. Samuel then gives Saul three signs to confirm that the Lord's word is true: he will meet two men near Rachel's tomb who will tell him the donkeys have been found and his father is worried about him; he will meet three men going up to Bethel who will give him two loaves of bread; and he will meet a procession of prophets coming down from the high place, with harp, tambourine, flute, and lyre before them, prophesying — and the Spirit of the Lord will come on Saul in power and he will prophesy with them and be changed into a different person.

All three signs are fulfilled in sequence. The most significant is the third: the Spirit of God comes on Saul and he prophesies with the prophets. The bystanders who knew Saul ask the famous question: "Is Saul also among the prophets?" — a proverbial question that captures the astonishment at the transformation. The same man who was searching for donkeys yesterday is now under prophetic inspiration today. But the transformation has limits: when his uncle asks Saul what Samuel told him, Saul says only that Samuel told him the donkeys had been found. He says nothing about the kingship. Keil and Delitzsch observe the contrast between the prophetic openness of Saul's experience at Gibeah and the closed reserve about his actual calling — he discloses neither the anointing nor the kingdom prophecy to his family.

John Gill notes that the flask of oil poured on Saul's head is the first royal anointing recorded in Israel's history. The word mashiach (anointed) that Hannah used in her song (2:10) now has a human referent for the first time. Matthew Henry reflects that Saul's becoming "a different person" when the Spirit comes on him is a genuine transformation — not permanent sanctification but a real infusion of prophetic gifts and kingly capacity. The tragedy is that the Spirit who came upon Saul will later depart from him (1 Sam 16:14).
1.historical_context: Royal anointing with oil was practiced widely in the ancient Near East as the ceremony of installation for kings and priests. The Amarna letters attest to Egyptian pharaohs anointing vassal kings; the Hebrew practice adapts this ceremony to signify divine rather than human appointment. The three signs Samuel gives Saul function within the ancient prophetic tradition of confirmatory signs: the prophet's word is validated by observable events. The procession of prophets playing instruments and prophesying together reflects a form of prophetic community (bnei haneviim, "sons of the prophets") that appears throughout Samuel and Kings. The ecstatic element — prophesying under the Spirit's influence — is associated with this communal prophetic tradition rather than with the solitary visionary prophecy of figures like Isaiah or Jeremiah.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured around the three signs, each of which is narrated as it occurs. The climactic third sign — prophesying with the prophets — receives the most attention because it produces the famous proverb. The closing scene at home, where Saul withholds information about the kingdom, provides a revealing character note: Saul is already managing what others know about his calling, which will become a pattern. The section closes with Saul returning to Gibeah, the royal future still hidden.
1.theological_application: Calvin reads the three confirmatory signs as evidence of God's pastoral care for the uncertain: the Lord does not merely command trust but provides visible evidence to strengthen faith. The signs confirm to Saul that what Samuel told him is true before Saul must act on it publicly. For those who receive callings that seem too large, the pattern of confirmatory signs — small verifiable events that authenticate the larger claim — is God's way of building faith step by step. The proverb "Is Saul also among the prophets?" teaches that the Spirit's transformation often surprises even those who know the person well. What human talent and background do not predict, the Spirit can supply suddenly and unmistakably.
1.christology: The anointing of Saul is the first occurrence in Israel's history of a human king being anointed with oil as the Lord's appointee. The term mashiach — anointed — which Hannah's song used prophetically now has its first concrete historical referent. But Saul is a type who will fail, pointing forward to the true anointed one. Jesus is anointed at his baptism not with oil but with the Spirit descending like a dove (Matt 3:16), fulfilling Isaiah's vision of the Spirit resting on the servant-king (Isa 11:2; Isa 61:1). Where Saul's Spirit-possession is temporary and ultimately revoked, Christ receives the Spirit without measure (John 3:34) and permanently. Where Saul's transformation into "a different person" is real but not lasting, the new creation that Christ inaugurates — "if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come" (2 Cor 5:17) — is definitive and permanent.

17.synthesis:
Samuel assembles Israel at Mizpah and delivers a prophetic indictment before the selection: "You have rejected your God, who saves you out of all your calamities and distresses. And you have said, 'No, appoint a king over us.'" The theological verdict is restated even as the process of selection proceeds — God's concession to their request does not mean his endorsement of it. The selection by lot is conducted systematically: first the tribe of Benjamin is chosen, then the clan of Matri, then Saul son of Kish. But when they look for him, Saul cannot be found. He is hiding among the baggage. The Lord reveals where he is; they bring him out; and he stands among the people, taller than anyone — "from his shoulders and up he was taller than any of the people." Samuel presents him: "Do you see the man the Lord has chosen? There is no one like him among all the people." And the people shout: "Long live the king!"

The scene of Saul hiding among the baggage has provoked different interpretations. John Gill suggests genuine humility — Saul was not seeking the kingship and was overwhelmed by the public selection. Matthew Henry is less charitable: the hiding could also reflect anxiety and avoidance of a responsibility he knew would expose him. Keil and Delitzsch take the most complex view: Saul's hiding is not deceit but a genuinely mixed psychological state — drawn toward the role, terrified by it, perhaps uncertain whether his private anointing was really what he thought it was. What is certain is that the contrast between his hiding and his eventual presentation is deliberate: the king must be found and brought out, not self-presenting.

The chapter closes with a note of division: valiant men whose hearts God had touched accompanied Saul home to Gibeah, but some troublemakers despised him and brought him no gifts. Samuel writes down the rights and duties of kingship in a document and deposits it before the Lord — a crucial detail establishing that the monarchy operates within a written covenant framework, not by royal prerogative alone.
17.historical_context: The selection by sacred lot (goral) was a standard method for determining divine choice in Israel — used for distributing land (Josh 18:6), for identifying guilty parties (Josh 7:14), and for priestly assignments (1 Chr 24:5). The Urim and Thummim, the oracular objects worn by the high priest, were likely the mechanism here. The crowd acclamation "Long live the king!" (yehi ha-melek) is the standard coronation formula, paralleling similar ancient Near Eastern inauguration shouts. Samuel's recording of the "rights and duties of kingship" (10:25) before the Lord connects directly to the Deuteronomic requirement that the king write and read a copy of the Torah (Deut 17:18–20), establishing that Israelite monarchy is constitutional — bounded by written law — from its very inception.
17.literary_structure: The section moves from indictment to selection to presentation to reception — a coronation sequence interrupted by the comic (or tragic) detail of the king hiding among the baggage. Samuel's indictment at the opening frames the entire ceremony: what follows is not a triumph but a concession. The crowd's enthusiastic acclamation and the quiet opposition of the troublemakers at the close establish the ambivalence that will characterize Saul's reign throughout.
17.theological_application: Calvin draws from Samuel's public indictment before the selection ceremony an important principle: the church's leaders are not absolved from declaring the truth about the people's condition even while accommodating their wishes under God's directive. Samuel does not quietly install the king the people wanted; he first states clearly what the request for a king meant theologically, then proceeds. This models prophetic integrity within political accommodation. The hiding king is a parable of reluctance before God's call that leadership demands — the call comes to those who are found, sometimes literally, trying to avoid it. God brings Saul out; God will also bring his servants to the roles they are called to whether or not they present themselves willingly.
17.christology: Saul's acclamation by some and rejection by others at his public presentation prefigures the divided reception of Christ throughout his ministry. "Long live the king!" and "this fellow will never rescue us!" (10:27) are the two voices that greet Israel's anointed, and they greet Christ's anointed as well. The Palm Sunday crowd and the Sanhedrin represent the same polarity: those whose hearts God has touched respond in loyalty, while others despise and withhold honor. Samuel's written charter of the kingdom, deposited before the Lord, anticipates the new covenant inaugurated by Christ — a covenant whose terms are written not on stone or scroll but on the heart (Jer 31:33; Heb 8:10).
